behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives entirely within the handler's own logic — compute HMAC-SHA256 over raw body, compare with header, return 401 if mismatch. The 'not forwarded' assertion is satisfied by checking a queue stub was never called. No cross-module wiring is needed to expose this bug; a crafted request object plus a queue stub is sufficient. REAL-INFRA: No. LLM-DEP: No. INTEGRATION-CHECK down-test: call the handler function with a wrong-signature request and a stub queue — the 401 path and the no-enqueue assertion are fully reproduced in one function call.
behaviors.test_description: Call the webhook handler function directly with a crafted request whose X-Signature-256 header is an HMAC of a different payload (not the actual body). Assert the return value is a 401 and that the queue stub's write/enqueue method was never invoked.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is about ordering across two module boundaries: the queue-write module and the HTTP response module. A pure unit test of the handler function cannot reliably distinguish 'wrote then responded' from 'responded then wrote' because both look like sequential calls from inside the function. The ordering guarantee requires observing the queue write completing relative to the response being committed, which means the queue module and the response path must both be wired in. REAL-INFRA: No real network needed — an in-process HTTP test client (e.g., supertest) is sufficient. LLM-DEP: No. INTEGRATION-CHECK: The down-test fails — a single-function call with a stub does not give you a reliable ordering observable; the up-test does not require real network. Integration survives.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a real (or realistic in-memory) queue implementation and drive it via an in-process HTTP client (e.g., supertest). Instrument the queue's write method to record a timestamp and instrument the HTTP response to record its timestamp. Send a valid webhook request and assert that the queue write timestamp precedes the response timestamp.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode (handler awaiting downstream before responding) is only observable when the downstream processor and the response path are both wired into the same execution. A unit test of the handler alone cannot show that an await on downstream processing is blocking the response — that requires a downstream module that actually takes time and a response path that can be observed independently. REAL-INFRA: No external network needed; a slow in-process downstream stub with a real timer is enough. LLM-DEP: No. INTEGRATION-CHECK: Down-test fails — you cannot observe the response-before-completion ordering from inside a single function call without wiring the downstream. Up-test does not require real network or OS-level concurrency. Integration survives.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a downstream processor stub that does not resolve for at least 2 seconds (e.g., a promise that never settles during the test). Send a valid request via an in-process HTTP client and assert that the HTTP response is received in well under 2 seconds, and that the downstream stub has not yet completed at the time the response arrives.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure requires real concurrency: a slow earlier request must be actively processing while a later request arrives and must not block it. This cross-request concurrency is invisible at Unit level. REAL-INFRA: Real wall-clock timing and real async concurrency (Node.js event loop) are required, but a real OS-level HTTP server is not — Node.js's in-process HTTP testing (supertest) preserves actual event-loop concurrency and real timer resolution, which is the relevant execution environment. LLM-DEP: No. INTEGRATION-CHECK: Down-test fails — you cannot observe cross-request blocking inside a single-function call. Up-test: real OS-level networking is not required; in-process concurrency is sufficient. Integration survives, though System is a reasonable escalation if the team needs load-level fidelity.
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler in-process (supertest). Fire a first request whose downstream processing stub sleeps for 10 seconds (non-blocking async). While it is still pending, fire a second request and measure wall-clock time from send to response using Date.now(). Assert the second response completes in under 3 seconds. Assert both requests eventually complete without error.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Deduplication requires shared state across two separate request invocations — the handler must remember that event ID X was seen on request 1 before processing request 2. That state lives in a dedup store (in-memory map, Redis, DB). The failure (double enqueue) is only observable when both the handler logic and the state store are wired together across two calls. A single-invocation unit test cannot reveal the bug. REAL-INFRA: No external network needed if the dedup store is local. LLM-DEP: No. INTEGRATION-CHECK: Down-test fails — single-call isolation cannot show cross-invocation state. Up-test: no real network required. Integration survives.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a real (or realistic in-memory) dedup store and queue implementation. Send an identical valid webhook request (same event ID, same body, valid signature) twice in sequence. Assert the queue's write method was called exactly once and the second request receives the same 200 acknowledgment without a second enqueue.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is entirely within the handler's error-handling branch: if the queue write throws or rejects with a 'queue full' signal, the handler must set status 503 and include Retry-After. The queue's 'full' state is simply an input signal — it can be reproduced by a stub that throws the appropriate error. No cross-module wiring is needed. REAL-INFRA: No. LLM-DEP: No. INTEGRATION-CHECK down-test: call the handler with a valid signature and a queue stub that rejects with a 'capacity exceeded' error; assert the response is 503 with a Retry-After header. This fully reproduces the failure in one function call.
behaviors.test_description: Call the webhook handler directly with a valid request and a queue stub configured to throw a 'queue full' / capacity-exceeded error on write. Assert the returned HTTP status is 503 and that the response includes a Retry-After header with a sensible positive integer value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This behavior tests the queue module itself, not the handler. The enforcement mechanism — checking current depth against capacity and rejecting writes that would exceed it — lives inside the queue's implementation. Mocking the queue would hide the very failure being tested. The real queue module must be exercised. REAL-INFRA: If the queue persists to a local file or in-memory structure, no real network is needed; local I/O or in-process state is sufficient (Integration). If it wraps an external broker (Redis, RabbitMQ) an actual instance would be System. In the most conservative interpretation that avoids assuming external infra, local in-process or local-disk persistence is sufficient. LLM-DEP: No. INTEGRATION-CHECK: Up-test — the real queue module must be wired, not mocked. Integration (or System if external broker) survives.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the persistent queue with a capacity of N (e.g., 3). Enqueue N events and assert all succeed. Attempt to enqueue one more event and assert the queue rejects it (throws or returns an error indicating capacity exceeded). Verify the queue's reported size remains N and previously enqueued events are unaffected.
